videoTitle: They Fired the "Dinosaur" to Save $150k, but the Code Had a Licensing Cost.
videoDescription:
They Fired the "Dinosaur" to Save $150k, but the Code Had a Licensing Cost.

When Apex Retail Logistics hires a ruthless young VP of IT obsessed with cutting costs, veteran DevOps Architect Frank Miller is labeled an overpaid dinosaur. The VP fires Frank and fabricates performance issues to deny him his severance and stock options, replacing him with a cheap offshore team. But they overlook a critical detail: the core automation software, NexusDeploy, is owned by Frank's LLC and licensed to the company. When the licensing handshake expires on Monday morning and Frank's HSM token is missing, the entire shipping system locks down, halting traffic at the Port of Los Angeles. As demurrage fees skyrocket and insurance adjusters refuse to cover the losses due to willful IP violations, ARL must face the financial reality of contract breach, leading to a massive $650,000 settlement.

🔥Legal Disclaimer:
 
Citing US Copyright Act (Title 17 of the United States Code) regarding the ownership of proprietary software and the illegality of unauthorized reverse-engineering of compiled binary code. [1976: 17 U.S.C. § 106 & § 501.]

Citing Delaware General Corporation Law (DGCL) regarding corporate contract compliance and enforcement of software licensing agreements under corporate governance. [2020: Title 8, Chapter 1, Section 121.]

Citing California Labor Code regarding wrongful termination and the illegality of fabricating cause to avoid paying earned severance packages. [2021: Cal. Lab. Code § 2922 & § 201.]

videoTitle: Kubernetes Ingress vs Gateway API on AWS EKS with Hands-on #devops #aws #ingress #gateway
videoDescription:
Kubernetes Ingress vs Gateway API on AWS EKS

Github Repo : https://github.com/RohanRusta21/mern-k8s.git

Timestamp:
Understand Concept : 00:00
Nginx Ingress on EKS cluster : 21:17
Nginx Fabric Gateway on EKS cluster : 28:00

1. NGINX Ingress Controller
What is NGINX Ingress?

Ingress is a Kubernetes resource that manages external HTTP/HTTPS access to services in a cluster.
NGINX Ingress Controller is an implementation of the Kubernetes Ingress specification using NGINX as the reverse proxy and load balancer.
It dynamically configures NGINX to route traffic to the appropriate services based on rules defined in Ingress resources.


Key Features

Traffic Routing: Routes external traffic to services using hostnames, paths, or headers.
Load Balancing: Distributes traffic across pods to improve performance and reliability.
SSL/TLS Termination: Handles HTTPS traffic and certificate management.
Dynamic Configuration: Automatically updates NGINX configuration when Ingress rules change.
High Availability: Supports multiple replicas for fault tolerance.


How It Works

Ingress Resource: Define routing rules (e.g., example.com/api → api-service).
Ingress Controller: Watches for changes to Ingress resources and updates NGINX configuration.
NGINX: Acts as a reverse proxy, forwarding requests to the correct backend services.


Use Cases

Exposing multiple services under a single domain.
Managing SSL certificates for secure communication.
Load balancing and traffic management in microservices architectures.


2. NGINX Fabric Gateway
What is NGINX Fabric Gateway?

NGINX Fabric Gateway is a component of the NGINX Service Mesh (part of the NGINX Controller ecosystem).
It acts as a sidecar proxy for services in a service mesh, managing communication between services.
Unlike Ingress, which focuses on north-south traffic (external to internal), Fabric Gateway manages east-west traffic (service-to-service within the cluster).


Key Features

Service Discovery: Dynamically locates services in the mesh.
Traffic Management: Implements retries, timeouts, and circuit breaking.
Security: Enforces mutual TLS (mTLS) for secure service-to-service communication.
Observability: Provides metrics, logs, and traces for monitoring.


How It Works

Sidecar Deployment: Runs alongside each service pod.
Traffic Interception: Captures all inbound and outbound traffic for the service.
Policy Enforcement: Applies routing, security, and resilience policies.


Use Cases

Securing and managing internal service communication.
Implementing canary deployments and A/B testing.
Monitoring and troubleshooting microservices interactions.

videoTitle: Ignition 8.3 Demo: DevOps - Version Control, Deployment Modes, REST API
videoDescription:
Ignition 8.3 introduces powerful DevOps capabilities that make it easier than ever to version, deploy, and manage your Ignition systems. Inductive Automation Chief Technology Evangelist Travis Cox explains how the new file-based configuration model enables seamless Git integration, how Deployment Modes support multiple environment setups, and how the new built-in REST API gives external tools full access to the Ignition Gateway.

FEATURE HIGHLIGHTS
- All Ignition configuration files are now accessible in the file system
- Native Git version control support for projects and gateway configuration
- Environmental overrides with deployment modes
- REST API is now built into Ignition’s gateway web UI
- Full API docs are available via OpenAPI
- Role-based access control for secure API interaction

LIVE DEMO HIGHLIGHTS
- Initializing Git in the data directory and pushing to GitLab
- Viewing JSON project and configuration files directly in Ignition folders
- Making changes and tracking them through GitHub Desktop
- Creating Deployment Modes for dev/test/prod with configuration inheritance
- Viewing mode-specific overrides in Git and the file system
- Using the REST API with tools like Postman and Somnia
- Viewing, adding, and configuring devices and DB connections through the API
- Accessing gateway metrics programmatically through API calls
- Dynamically creating new devices through a POST request
--------------------------------------------------

videoTitle: Authentic DevOps with AI Foundry + GitHub, on Sip & Sync with Azure
videoDescription:
Join us for another exciting episode of Sip and Sync with Azure! ☁️🎤 Host Katie Novotny welcomes Krystal Folkes, Software Global Black Belt at Microsoft, to explore how Azure AI Foundry agents can revolutionize DevOps workflows by integrating with GitHub and GitHub Copilot.
In this hands-on demo, Krystal walks through building a security-focused agent using MCP tools, connecting to GitHub's advanced security advisories, and orchestrating multi-agent workflows—all from a local machine. Whether you're a DevOps engineer, security lead, or developer curious about AI-powered automation, this episode is packed with insights to elevate your developer experience.

What You'll Learn:

- How to build a security agent using Azure AI Foundry
- Connecting GitHub MCP tools to automate vulnerability insights
- Using GitHub Copilot to fine-tune prompts for agent instructions
- Running agents locally with secure GitHub token authentication
- Orchestrating multi-agent workflows for DevOps, planning, and documentation
- Leveraging Azure Key Vault and managed identity for secure integrations

👨‍🦲 Quem é Marcelo Neves?

Marcelo Neves é o fundador do CANAL VALOR e trabalha com liderança em desenvolvimento de produtos digitais desde 1993. Nos anos 90 liderou o desenvolvimento de produtos B2C voltados para a saúde.  Nos anos 2000 esteve envolvido na liderança de produtos para indústrias farmacêuticas, produtos de automação de força de vendas e de gestão de relacionamento com clientes (CRM). Em todos esses anos participou como desenvolvedor, líder de time de produto, mentor e empreendedor na construção de produtos digitais, atendendo empresas como IBGE, Sanofi Aventis, Pfizer, Yduqs, Banco Votorantin e vários outras.

videoTitle: HOW TO CREATE A VARIABLE GROUP ON AZURE DEVOPS
videoDescription:
Variables give you a convenient way to get key bits of data into various parts of the pipeline. The most common use of variables is to define a value that you can then use in your pipeline. All variables are stored as strings and are mutable. The value of a variable can change from run to run or job to job of your pipeline.

OUTLINE

00:00 Go to "Pipelines"
00:04 Click on "Library"
00:08 Click on "Variable Group"
00:14 Define the variable group name
00:28 Add the variables
00:58 Click on "Save"

videoTitle: How to Fix High CPU Usage in Production | DevOps Real-Time Scenario (Day 3)
videoDescription:
💥 Day 3 of the DevOps in ARIA Series – Real-Time Scenarios!

🎯 Today’s Interview Question:
“Your production server is showing high CPU usage — what will you do?”

This is more than an interview question — it’s a real-world DevOps challenge you’ll face on the job.

🎥 In this video, I break down:

✅ How to investigate CPU issues using tools like top, htop, Grafana, and Prometheus

✅ How to identify which process or service is consuming resources

✅ When to restart a service — and what to check before and after

✅ How code bugs or deployment issues can spike CPU

✅ When to scale infra or adjust auto-scaling policies

✅ Why documenting and learning from incidents makes you a stronger engineer

💡 Clarity, calmness, and a systematic approach — that’s the DevOps way!

videoTitle: Neurodiversity and the Essence of DevOps - Jeff Sussna
videoDescription:
It’s been gratifying to see the DevOps community take up the challenge of making empathy the essence of what we do. We should be proud of ourselves as an example to other organizational transformation approaches. We still, though, have room to grow. Whether it’s NoOps, or cloud-native chauvinism, or labeling people on the autism spectrum as lacking the capacity for empathy, we create subtle separations and exclusions through language and practice.

I would like to use my experience as a person with autism to reflect on the true meaning of empathy as the essence of DevOps. People on the spectrum are typically viewed as lacking something. In the process, our strengths and value get ignored. Real empathy means looking for wisdom and capability across differences. How do we do that in a comprehensive way? What are the obstacles to doing so in our current approaches to DevOps? How can overcoming those obstacles provide a model for digital transformation, and for healthy organizations, that transcends dev and ops?

https://www.devopsdays.org/events/2018-london/program/jeff-sussna/
channelDescription: Videos from DevOpsDays London

videoTitle: Kubernetes at Mercedes-Benz | Philipp Bormann & Jens Erat | The DEVOPS Conference - Copenhagen, 2022
videoDescription:
How did Kubernetes change the game for software engineers at Mercedes-Benz?

Years ago, software engineers faced hard times at Mercedes-Benz: spreadsheet operations, manual processes, grown infrastructure, and strict governance. A grassroots initiative of engineers accepted the challenge to change the game – and their silver bullet was Kubernetes.

Join us on our journey from introducing Kubernetes 0.9 on managed servers to an on-premises self-service cloud platform with close to 1000 clusters on Cluster API. You will learn about our stake in transforming a data center with a young team that mostly did not know enterprise processes before.

We will take you back on our journey, including some technical details about the challenges we faced, our solutions to those, and lessons learned on the way.
---
Speakers:

Philipp Bormann, Platform Architect at Mercedes-Benz Group AG

After 10 years as a Network Engineer, Philipp became a Platform Architect for on-premises cloud platforms at Mercedes-Benz Group. He is one of the founding members of the company’s largest Kubernetes platform that helps to transform Mercedes-Benz into a digital car manufacturer.
Find Philipp on LinkedIn: https://www.linkedin.com/in/philipp-bormann-x/

Jens Erat, DevOps Engineer at Mercedes-Benz Tech Innovation

Jens is a DevOps engineer and expert in advanced cloud wizardry. He has a strong passion for not only getting, but also keeping IT projects running. Having joined Mercedes-Benz's efforts in building a cloud-native platform in 2017, he experienced and formed the transition from enterprise processes to self-service and automation.
Find Jens on LinkedIn: https://www.linkedin.com/in/jens-erat/


---
👇Learn more:
https://www.thedevopsconference.com
https://www.eficode.com/services/devops-transformation?hsLang=en
https://www.eficode.com

channelDescription:
This is the Microsoft channel for videos related to the various DevOps tools, practices and services. Subscribe to learn more about the vast Microsoft technologies such as Azure DevOps, GitHub & GitHub Actions, cloud native technologies, all while delivering greater DevOps capabilities in Azure! 

DevOps is not only a product but a practice, a culture and a mindset. Most importantly, it is for everyone! videoTitle: Cracking the DevOps Code: Courses & Certifications for Success
videoDescription:
Take the courses mentioned here, get certifications and do not forget to add these using our AI-Powered Resume Builder (https://pro.kudoswall.com)
Certifications for Employed DevOps Professionals:
AWS Certified DevOps Engineer: This certification from Amazon Web Services (AWS) validates your expertise in deploying, operating, and managing applications on the AWS platform.
Azure DevOps Engineer: Offered by Microsoft, this certification demonstrates your skills in designing and implementing DevOps practices using Azure technologies.
Google Cloud Professional DevOps Engineer: This certification focuses on designing, developing, and managing DevOps practices on the Google Cloud Platform.
Certified Kubernetes Administrator (CKA): Certified by the Cloud Native Computing Foundation (CNCF), CKA validates your Kubernetes skills, which are crucial for container orchestration.
Certified Jenkins Engineer: Offered by CloudBees, this certification recognizes your proficiency in using Jenkins for continuous integration and continuous delivery (CI/CD) pipelines.
Red Hat Certified Engineer in DevOps: This certification from Red Hat validates your skills in using Red Hat technologies for automating, managing, and optimizing DevOps processes.

Courses for Aspiring DevOps Engineers:
Introduction to DevOps Practices: Online platforms like Coursera, edX, and Udemy offer various introductory courses that cover the fundamental concepts and practices of DevOps.
Docker and Kubernetes Courses: Understanding containerization and orchestration tools like Docker and Kubernetes is essential. There are numerous courses available for beginners and advanced users.
Cloud Platform Courses (AWS, Azure, GCP): Learning the basics of cloud platforms and their DevOps-related services is crucial. Many platforms offer free and paid courses for beginners.
CI/CD Pipeline Courses: Courses on continuous integration and continuous delivery tools like Jenkins, GitLab CI/CD, and CircleCI are essential for automating software delivery.
Infrastructure as Code (IaC) Courses: Learning tools like Terraform and Ansible for automating infrastructure deployment is key for DevOps engineers.
Monitoring and Logging Courses: Understanding tools like Prometheus, Grafana, and ELK Stack (Elasticsearch, Logstash, Kibana) for monitoring and logging is vital.
Scripting and Programming Courses: Proficiency in scripting languages like Python and programming languages like Java can enhance your DevOps skills.
Security and Compliance Courses: DevOps engineers should also be well-versed in security practices. Courses on DevSecOps and security automation can be beneficial.
#DevOpsIndia #Certifications #Courses #TechSkills #CareerGrowth #Cloud #Automation

videoTitle: Build an AI PR Reviewer with GitHub Actions (DevOps Tutorial - AWS Bedrock + Terraform)
videoDescription:
0:00 Intro – Why DevOps Needs AI Now
0:40 Demo – AI Reviewing a Pull Request with AWS Bedrock
1:08 Environment Setup (Terraform, AWS, GitHub, VS Code)
1:55 Creating the GitHub Repo with Terraform
3:12 Terraform Init & First Apply
3:53 Project Structure (infra/ + scripts/)
6:21 Creating S3 Backend & DynamoDB State Lock
8:21 Setting Up GitHub OIDC in AWS IAM
11:39 Terraform Plan & Apply (IAM + Bedrock Role)
12:26 Building the GitHub Actions Workflow
15:42 Writing the Python Bedrock Review Script
20:51 Testing the PR Workflow
21:58 Fixing “No Merge Base” GitHub Error
23:34 Debugging Bedrock Model Issues (Claude EOL)
24:40 Switching to Nova 2 Light Model
25:44 Manual Bedrock API Fix (Inference Config)
30:28 Improving Human-Readable Output
33:31 Successful AI PR Review Demo
33:50 Testing AI Against Insecure EC2 Example
35:50 AI Security Findings & Best Practice Feedback
36:47 Final Thoughts – AI Won’t Replace You (But You Must Adapt)

In this video, I build an AI-powered DevOps pull request reviewer using GitHub Actions, AWS Bedrock, and Terraform.

Instead of relying on manual code reviews, this pipeline automatically:

Assumes an AWS IAM role using OIDC (no static keys)

Extracts the pull request diff

Sends the changes to an AI model in AWS Bedrock

Posts a structured DevOps review directly on the PR

We’ll cover:

Terraform remote state best practices

GitHub OIDC federation with AWS

IAM trust policies

Bedrock model invocation

Automating infrastructure reviews with AI

This is what modern AI-integrated DevOps pipelines look like.

If you're a DevOps engineer, platform engineer, or cloud engineer looking to integrate AI into CI/CD, this build will give you a practical starting point.

🔔 Subscribe for more AI + DevOps + Terraform content
💬 Comment if you'd like to see this expanded into production-grade policy enforcement
🚀 Next: Blocking merges based on AI risk scoring

videoTitle: Bringing Security & DevOps Together | Making DevSecOps Easy
videoDescription:
DevOps engineers are increasingly becoming the ones that are responsible for maintaining the security of their infrastructure. And these environments are becoming more complex to manage.

Every technology needs a special hardening, a special secure configuration, and not every DevOps person has the special knowledge about the security configuration at every layer.

We want to give you answers to fix these problems easily and with confidence.

Mondoo is full stack security designed for DevOps teams. It fits into your workflow so you can test the infrastructure you're building from local development through your pipeline and all the way through into your runtime environments.

We give you a comprehensive view into your infrastructure no matter which layer you’re looking at. We help you understand what you have and what needs to be improved.

videoTitle: What is the Git Index? || #Git #GitHub #GitLab #DevOps #JavaScript #Python #Java #Bitbucket #GitGut
videoDescription:
Ever wonder what the Git index is and why we must use the git staging index and stage a file before we perform a commit?

Well, Git isn't like Mircosoft Word's autosave feature. You have to tell it which files to include in the next Git commit. That's called staging a file in Git.

So yes, you have to stage files first, and when you're happy with your code, you perform the Git commit, and all the staged files will become part of your Git commit history.

You can learn more about staging a Git file and the git add command here:

https://www.theserverside.com/blog/Coffee-Talk-Java-News-Stories-and-Opinions/git-add-index-stage-file-staging-commit-combine-untracked-staging-status

videoTitle: Real world DevOps project from start to finish - WireGuard VPN Part 1
videoDescription:
CHECK OUT MY DEVOPS COURSE WITH 85% OFF COUPON AT https://sre.rs

Hello everyone,


I've made a DevOps course covering a lot of different technologies and applications,
aimed at startups, small companies and individuals who want to self-host
their infrastructure.

This video shows the first part of setting up WireGuard VPN.

TL;DR:
Course page: https://www.udemy.com/course/real-world-devops-project-from-start-to-finish/?referralCode=9AC467077593D22878A2
Discount coupon is available at https://sre.rs

Details:
To get this out of the way - this course doesn't cover Kubernetes or similar -
I'm of the opinion that for startups, small companies, and especially individuals,
you probably don't need Kubernetes. Unless you have a whole DevOps team,
it usually brings more problems than benefits, and unnecessary infrastructure bills
buried a lot of startups before they got anywhere.

As for prerequisites, you can't be a complete beginner in the world of computers.
If you've never even heard of Docker, if you don't know at least something about DNS,
or if you don't have any experience with Linux, this course is probably not for you.
That being said, I do explain the basics too, but probably not in enough detail
for a complete beginner.

I encourage you to watch "free preview" videos to get the sense of what
will be covered, but here's the gist:

The goal of the course is to create an easily deployable and reproducible server
which will have "everything" a startup or a small company will need - VPN, mail, Git,
CI/CD, messaging, hosting websites and services, sharing files, calendar, etc.
It can also be useful to individuals who want to self-host all of those - I ditched Google 99.9%
and other than that being a good feeling, I'm not worried that some AI bug will lock my account
with no one to talk to about resolving the issue.

Considering that it covers a wide variety of topics, it doesn't go in depth in any of those.
Think of it as going down a highway towards the end destination, but on the way there I show you
all the junctions where I think it's useful to do more research on the subject.

We'll deploy services inside Docker and LXC (Linux Containers). Those will include
a mail server (iRedMail), Zulip (Slack and Microsoft Teams alternative), GitLab (with GitLab Runner and CI/CD),
Nextcloud (file sharing, calendar, contacts, etc.), checkmk (monitoring solution), Pi-hole (ad blocking on DNS level),
Traefik with Docker and file providers (a single HTTP/S entry point with automatic routing and TLS certificates).

We'll set up WireGuard, a modern and fast VPN solution for secure access to VPS' internal network,
and I'll also show you how to get a wildcard TLS certificate with certbot and DNS provider.

To wrap it all up, we'll write a simple Python application that will compare a list of the desired backups
with the list of finished backups, and send a result to a Zulip stream.
We'll write the application, do a 'git push' to GitLab which will trigger a CI/CD pipeline
that will build a Docker image, push it to a private registry, and then,
with the help of the GitLab runner, run it on the VPS and post a result to a Zulip stream with a webhook.


If you apply the coupon, I'd appreciate if you leave your feedback on Udemy
after you're done and spread the word about the course. It would mean a lot.


I hope that you find it useful.


Happy learning,
Predrag

videoTitle: Master IaC with Azure DevOps (C# + Azure Infrastructure Guide) | Workshop
videoDescription:
Learn how to automate Azure infrastructure with Azure DevOps and Pulumi — using C# and Infrastructure as Code (IaC).

This hands-on workshop walks you through setting up an Azure DevOps pipeline that automatically provisions real Azure resources with Pulumi, a modern Infrastructure as Code platform. You’ll use C# to define, deploy, and manage infrastructure safely — no ARM templates or portal clicks required.

By the end, you’ll understand how to connect Azure DevOps with Pulumi, apply Infrastructure as Code best practices, and continuously deliver cloud infrastructure through a real DevOps workflow.

✅ Build and configure a full Azure DevOps pipeline that deploys real Azure infrastructure
✅ Use C# and Pulumi to manage cloud infrastructure as code
✅ Automate provisioning — no manual portal steps or ARM templates
✅ Preview and approve every change before deployment
✅ Commit, push, and let your cloud update itself automatically

▬▬▬ 📌 Resources ▬▬▬
🎓 Register for upcoming workshops: https://pulumip.us/Upcoming-Workshops
🚀 Get Started with Pulumi: https://pulumip.us/Get-Started_Azure
🤖 Try Pulumi Neo: https://www.pulumi.com/product/neo/
💬 Talk with us: https://slack.pulumi.com/
channelDescription:
Pulumi is an open-source infrastructure-as-code platform that lets you build, deploy, and manage cloud infrastructure with programming languages like TypeScript, Python, Go, C#, and Java, instead of domain-specific languages like HCL or YAML.

With Pulumi, you can bring everything your chosen language has to offer — loops, conditionals, type checking, error handling, test frameworks, package ecosystems, and more — to the infrastructure domain. And because it’s real code, AI agents love it as much as humans do.

videoTitle: I Turned Claude Code Into My DevOps For OpenClaw
videoDescription:
I wanted OpenClaw on my VPS without having to babysit anything, or care about service restart, config, gateway issus, and upgrades  myself.

So I built an OpenClaw DevOps Kit that gives Claude Code the playbooks it needs to be a DevOps operator on the server. It can bootstrap the VPS, install Claude Code if needed, bring in the OpenClaw playbooks, install and configure OpenClaw, set up alerts, help with security checks, keep an eye on the gateway, and repair things when needed.

The watchdog repo is open source and contains the core functionality that does most of the fuctionality. My personal, customzied version with the broader setup flow that I personally like and extra playbooks is on Ko-Fi.

Get my customized OpenClaw DevOps Kit:
https://ko-fi.com/s/8752f1ccc7

Open source Watchdog Repo:
https://github.com/MichaelZelbel/openclaw-claude-code-devops-watchdog

More Practical AI experiments are coming soon.

videoTitle: Bash Scripting for DevOps Engineers | Automate Kubernetes, Disk & Log Monitoring
videoDescription:
Bash scripting is one of the most powerful skills every DevOps engineer should know.

In real DevOps environments, engineers repeatedly check things like:
• Is the Kubernetes pod running?
• Is the disk almost full?
• Are there errors in system logs?

Doing this manually once is fine — but doing it every day is not scalable.

This is where bash scripting helps automate repetitive operational tasks.

In this tutorial, you’ll learn the fundamentals of shell scripting and then apply them to real DevOps automation examples used in production environments.

🔧 What You’ll Learn

• How to create and execute a shell script
• Understanding the shebang (#!/bin/bash)
• Setting executable permissions with chmod +x
• Using variables in bash scripting
• Writing if-else conditions for automation decisions
• Using for loops in shell scripts
• Using exit codes for automation workflows

🚀 Real DevOps Automation Scripts

✔ Kubernetes Pod Health Check Script (kubectl + JSONPath)
✔ Disk Usage Monitoring Script with threshold alerts (df, awk, sed)
✔ Real-time Log Monitoring Script using tail -f and grep

These examples demonstrate how small scripts can automate daily DevOps operations like monitoring services, detecting failures, and preventing production issues.

12:35 - Script #2: Monitor Disk Usage Automatically
16:09 - Script #3: Detect Errors in Log Files
18:44 - How Small Scripts Power DevOps Automation

videoTitle: 5 Core SRE Topics Every Interviewer Will Ask About #devops #sre
videoDescription:
If you're preparing for an SRE interview, here are 5 core topics you must master:

First — SLI, SLO, SLA.
Understand how reliability is actually measured in production. Not definitions — real metrics, error budgets, and what they mean during incidents.
https://youtu.be/CleIOnYMl1Y

Second — Distributed Tracing with Prometheus, Grafana, Tempo, and OTEL.
Learn how a request flows across services. This is what interviewers expect when they ask about microservices debugging.
https://youtu.be/MUX4lYBdmQU

Third — Metrics with Prometheus and OpenTelemetry.
Know how your application exposes data — CPU, memory, request latency — and how you visualize it in Grafana.
https://youtu.be/B_muisnbf78

Fourth — Centralized Logging with Loki.
Logs are your ground truth. Learn how to aggregate and query logs across services during failures.
https://youtu.be/sCQcvGrsv_E

Fifth — Production Monitoring for a Spring Boot App.
Put everything together — metrics, logs, tracing — full observability stack like real SREs use.
https://youtu.be/k7bzxk1QtF8

If you can explain and demo these, you're already ahead of most candidates.

videoTitle: DevOps Realnosti: Dobre i loše strane koje niko ne pominje
videoDescription:
DevOps je postao standard u modernom IT svetu, ali često se zaboravlja da ova metodologija dolazi sa sopstvenim setom prednosti i izazova. U ovom videu Katarina i Gorana analiziraju DevOps kroz prizmu realnih projekata, bez ulepšavanja i teorijskih definicija.

Govorimo o onome što DevOps zaista donosi kada se primeni u produkciji. S jedne strane imamo povećanu brzinu isporuke softvera, bolju saradnju timova i stabilnost sistema, ali s druge strane postoji kompleksnost, visoki zahtevi za znanjem i otpor prema promenama kulture unutar firme.

Ovaj razgovor je namenjen svima koji žele da razumeju šta zaista znači usvojiti DevOps kulturu. Bilo da ste developer, sistemski administrator ili menadžer koji odlučuje o tehnološkom pravcu kompanije, važno je znati gde su granice automatizacije i gde počinju ljudski i organizacioni izazovi. Delimo lekcije koje smo naučile radeći na kompleksnim sistemima i objašnjavamo zašto DevOps za neke timove postaje prednost, dok za druge predstavlja nepotrebnu komplikaciju.

Pretplatite se na kanal za više dubinskih analiza IT trendova i saveta za razvoj karijere i poslovanja u tehnološkom sektoru.

Napišite nam u komentarima koji je po vama najveći problem ili najveća korist koju ste iskusili radeći u DevOps okruženju.

videoTitle: Why DevOps Engineers Should Learn AI Infrastructure
videoDescription:
Why DevOps Engineers Should Learn AI Infrastructure (MLOps, AI Agents & Kubernetes)

Most DevOps engineers today are learning Kubernetes, CI/CD, observability, cloud platforms, and platform engineering.

But another major shift is happening underneath all of it: AI Infrastructure.

Behind every ChatGPT response, AI Agent, RAG application, recommendation engine, and LLM-powered system is a complex infrastructure stack involving GPUs, Kubernetes, model serving, inference systems, vector databases, observability, and distributed systems.

The interesting part?
Many of the skills required to build and operate these systems already overlap with what DevOps engineers have been doing for years.

In this video, you'll learn:

✅ Why AI Infrastructure is growing so rapidly
✅ Training vs Inference explained
✅ Why inference is the real production workload
✅ GPUs and Kubernetes for AI workloads
✅ Model Serving and AI Platforms
✅ RAG, Embeddings & Vector Databases
✅ AI Observability and Monitoring
✅ AI Agents as Distributed Systems
✅ ML Engineer vs MLOps vs AI Infrastructure Engineer vs Platform Engineer
✅ Why DevOps Engineers are uniquely positioned for this opportunity

▬▬▬▬▬▬▬ Timestamps ▬▬▬▬▬▬▬
00:00 - Why DevOps Engineers Should Care About AI Infrastructure
00:56 - AI Changed Infrastructure Forever
01:48 - Training vs Inference (What Actually Runs in Production)
03:16 - GPUs, Kubernetes & AI Workloads
04:25 - How Kubernetes Serves AI Models
05:28 - Why DevOps Engineers Have an Advantage
05:50 - RAG, Embeddings & Vector Databases Explained
07:04 - Why AI Observability Is Hard
07:46 - AI Agents Are Distributed Systems
09:16 - ML Engineer vs MLOps vs AI Infra vs Platform Engineer
11:06 - The AI Infrastructure Opportunity for DevOps Engineers

Series: From DevOps to AI Engineering

In this series, we'll explore AI from a systems engineering perspective, including:

• AI Infrastructure
• MLOps & LLMOps
• AI Agents
• Kubernetes for AI
• GPUs & Model Serving
• Vector Databases
• RAG Systems
• AI Observability
• Production AI Architectures

If you're a DevOps, Platform, SRE, Cloud, or Infrastructure Engineer looking to understand where AI is heading, this series is for you.

videoTitle: Stop using Read-Host and do this instead #powershell  #devops  #scripting
videoDescription:
Stop using Read-Host and do this instead:

Read-Host has limitations. 

- You cannot pass information to it 
- You cannot validate what the user provided
- It always stops the script

There is a better way - use parameters instead.

- Control the type of input
- Make it mandatory or optional
- Pass argument from the command line
- or prompt the user to provide an argument, when required and not passed

In this short video, I'll show you how to implement simple parameters and how to use them.

Would you like to learn more about the parameters?

videoTitle: Improving DevOps Security and Efficiency at Cathay with AWS ProServe | Amazon Web Services
videoDescription:
Cathay set out to better understand how cybersecurity was integrated across its development processes. With nearly 1,000 developers working across global teams, false positives and late-stage fixes were slowing innovation and increasing costs.

Working with AWS ProServe, Cathay assessed and modernized its DevOps workflows using AI-powered tools. The result was earlier detection of vulnerabilities, streamlined remediation, and more efficient development cycles—reducing costs by up to 80% and improving developer experience.

videoTitle: Day 25 – Infrastructure as Code (IaC) in Azure DevOps | 30 Days Azure DevOps Challenge
videoDescription:
🚀 Welcome to Day 25 of my 30 Days Azure DevOps Challenge!

🔹 Today’s topic: Infrastructure as Code (IaC)

In this session, I’ll explain what IaC is and why it’s a game-changer in the world of DevOps. Instead of manually configuring servers and networks, IaC allows us to define infrastructure in code and deploy it quickly, consistently, and at scale.

✅ You’ll learn:

What is Infrastructure as Code (IaC)?

Why IaC saves time and avoids errors

Popular tools like Terraform, ARM templates, and Bicep

Real-life analogy: IaC is like following a recipe 🧑‍🍳 for consistent results

✨ Key takeaway: Infrastructure as Code = Speed, Consistency, and Automation

📌 Next up in Day 26 → We’ll dive deeper into Terraform with Azure DevOps to see IaC in action.

videoTitle: GitHub vs GitLab: Which DevOps Platform is Best for Your Project?
videoDescription:
The ultimate fight for the modern developer workflow comes down to two giants: GitHub and GitLab. They both start with Git, but their fundamental philosophies on how you build, test, and deploy software couldn't be more different. Are you better off with GitHub's flexible "Best-of-Breed" Marketplace approach, or is GitLab's unified, "All-in-One" DevSecOps platform the right choice for your enterprise?

In this comprehensive comparison, I break down the core features, CI/CD platforms (GitHub Actions vs. GitLab CI/CD), security models, and hosting flexibility to help you make the definitive decision for your team, whether you're working on open-source, a complex enterprise pipeline, or a heavily regulated project.

Watch the full video to find out which platform is the absolute best fit for your specific needs!

What do you think? Which platform are you using for your current project and why? Let me know in the comments below!

If you enjoy the video,  like it, and subscribe to my channel!
See you in the next video!

videoTitle: D365 F&O: Setup an Azure DevOps CI Build Pipeline | Part 7
videoDescription:
Set up a CI pipeline for D365 in Azure DevOps. Learn to deploy a Build VM and automate your code builds for team development.

───────────
🕒 Timestamps
───────────

0:00 → Intro: Why We Need a CI Pipeline (Continuous Integration)
0:25 → Part 1: Create a Self-Hosted Agent Pool in ADO
1:19 → Part 2: Provision the Build VM in LCS
1:54 → Selecting the "Build and Test" Topology
2:24 → Advanced Settings: Linking the ADO Agent Pool & Branch
3:04 → Selecting the VM Size (Memory Optimized)
3:47 → Part 3: Configure the Build VM (Reset Password & Connect)
4:24 → Exploring the Build VM File Structure (SDK Folder)
4:53 → Part 4: Review the Auto-Generated Build Pipeline
5:43 → Enabling Continuous Integration Triggers & Path Filters
6:23 → Part 5: Branching Strategy (Rename 'dev' to 'Main')
6:52 → Connecting Visual Studio to Source Control (Team Explorer)
7:19 → Mapping a Local Workspace
7:55 → Adding the Local Project to Source Control
8:34 → Adding the axmodulesbuild File & Checking In
9:12 → Part 6: Creating a New 'dev' Branch from 'Main'
10:06 → Part 7: How the CI Build Process Works
10:23 → Reviewing the Build Stages (Build, Sync, Package)
10:59 → Under the Hood: Pipeline Tasks vs. VM Scripts
11:53 → Part 8: Reviewing the Final Build Artifact
12:27 → Recap

──────────────
💡 What You'll Learn
──────────────

✓ Create a self-hosted Agent Pool in Azure DevOps for D365 builds.
✓ How to deploy a Build VM from LCS and connect it to your pipeline.
✓ Configure an auto-generated build pipeline with CI triggers and path filters.
✓ Connect Visual Studio to Azure DevOps (TFVC) using Team Explorer.
✓ Check in your code to automatically trigger the build and create a Deployable Package.

──────────────────────────
🚀 About This D365 Development Series
──────────────────────────

This series goes beyond basic tutorials to show you how real-world Dynamics 365 (D365FO) projects are built from start to finish. We'll build a complete, end-to-end "Automated Customer Tiering System" for a fictional client, Bluestone Industrial Components (BIC).

You'll see every step of a production-ready solution, from setup to deployment, covering:
➤ Core X++ Development: Extending tables and forms (CustTable), creating Enums, and implementing custom business logic using Chain of Command (CoC).
➤ Automation: Building robust, automated batch jobs with the SysOperation Framework.
➤ APIs & Integration: Creating Data Entities to build secure OData REST APIs and for Data Management Framework (DMF) exports.
➤ Event-Driven Architecture: Firing real-time alerts from D365 to Azure Logic Apps using Business Events.
➤ Business Intelligence: Connecting D365 to Power BI to build an interactive sales dashboard.
➤ DevOps & ALM: Setting up a complete CI/CD pipeline in Azure DevOps, managing source control (TFVC), and automating builds.

────────────────
💻 Tools & Technologies
────────────────

🔹Dynamics 365 Finance & Operations (D365FO)
🔹X++
🔹Visual Studio 2019
🔹Lifecycle Services (LCS)
🔹Azure DevOps (ADO)
🔹SQL Server Management Studio (SSMS)
🔹Power BI Desktop
🔹Azure Logic Apps

───────────────────
💬 Feedback & Future Topics
───────────────────

This series covers the core components of a practical, end-to-end D365 project. I know there are many more topics we could dive into, from advanced debugging to complex form patterns.

I welcome your feedback and ideas! If there's a specific D365 topic you'd like to see covered in a future video, please share your suggestions in the comments section below.

#Dynamics365, #AzureDevOps, #ContinuousIntegration, #D365Development

videoTitle: They Fired the "Dinosaur" to Save $150k, but the Code Had a Licensing Cost.
videoDescription:
They Fired the "Dinosaur" to Save $150k, but the Code Had a Licensing Cost.

When Apex Retail Logistics hires a ruthless young VP of IT obsessed with cutting costs, veteran DevOps Architect Frank Miller is labeled an overpaid dinosaur. The VP fires Frank and fabricates performance issues to deny him his severance and stock options, replacing him with a cheap offshore team. But they overlook a critical detail: the core automation software, NexusDeploy, is owned by Frank's LLC and licensed to the company. When the licensing handshake expires on Monday morning and Frank's HSM token is missing, the entire shipping system locks down, halting traffic at the Port of Los Angeles. As demurrage fees skyrocket and insurance adjusters refuse to cover the losses due to willful IP violations, ARL must face the financial reality of contract breach, leading to a massive $650,000 settlement.

🔥Legal Disclaimer:
 
Citing US Copyright Act (Title 17 of the United States Code) regarding the ownership of proprietary software and the illegality of unauthorized reverse-engineering of compiled binary code. [1976: 17 U.S.C. § 106 & § 501.]

Citing Delaware General Corporation Law (DGCL) regarding corporate contract compliance and enforcement of software licensing agreements under corporate governance. [2020: Title 8, Chapter 1, Section 121.]

Citing California Labor Code regarding wrongful termination and the illegality of fabricating cause to avoid paying earned severance packages. [2021: Cal. Lab. Code § 2922 & § 201.]

🔥Watch more: https://youtu.be/htaWfs71oUw

#officedrama      #workplacerevenge      #karma      #toxicbosses     #officesurvival     #workplacejustice

videoTitle: Kubernetes Ingress vs Gateway API on AWS EKS with Hands-on #devops #aws #ingress #gateway
videoDescription:
Kubernetes Ingress vs Gateway API on AWS EKS

Github Repo : https://github.com/RohanRusta21/mern-k8s.git

Timestamp:
Understand Concept : 00:00
Nginx Ingress on EKS cluster : 21:17
Nginx Fabric Gateway on EKS cluster : 28:00

1. NGINX Ingress Controller
What is NGINX Ingress?

Ingress is a Kubernetes resource that manages external HTTP/HTTPS access to services in a cluster.
NGINX Ingress Controller is an implementation of the Kubernetes Ingress specification using NGINX as the reverse proxy and load balancer.
It dynamically configures NGINX to route traffic to the appropriate services based on rules defined in Ingress resources.


Key Features

Traffic Routing: Routes external traffic to services using hostnames, paths, or headers.
Load Balancing: Distributes traffic across pods to improve performance and reliability.
SSL/TLS Termination: Handles HTTPS traffic and certificate management.
Dynamic Configuration: Automatically updates NGINX configuration when Ingress rules change.
High Availability: Supports multiple replicas for fault tolerance.


How It Works

Ingress Resource: Define routing rules (e.g., example.com/api → api-service).
Ingress Controller: Watches for changes to Ingress resources and updates NGINX configuration.
NGINX: Acts as a reverse proxy, forwarding requests to the correct backend services.


Use Cases

Exposing multiple services under a single domain.
Managing SSL certificates for secure communication.
Load balancing and traffic management in microservices architectures.


2. NGINX Fabric Gateway
What is NGINX Fabric Gateway?

NGINX Fabric Gateway is a component of the NGINX Service Mesh (part of the NGINX Controller ecosystem).
It acts as a sidecar proxy for services in a service mesh, managing communication between services.
Unlike Ingress, which focuses on north-south traffic (external to internal), Fabric Gateway manages east-west traffic (service-to-service within the cluster).


Key Features

Service Discovery: Dynamically locates services in the mesh.
Traffic Management: Implements retries, timeouts, and circuit breaking.
Security: Enforces mutual TLS (mTLS) for secure service-to-service communication.
Observability: Provides metrics, logs, and traces for monitoring.


How It Works

Sidecar Deployment: Runs alongside each service pod.
Traffic Interception: Captures all inbound and outbound traffic for the service.
Policy Enforcement: Applies routing, security, and resilience policies.


Use Cases

Securing and managing internal service communication.
Implementing canary deployments and A/B testing.
Monitoring and troubleshooting microservices interactions.

videoTitle: Ignition 8.3 Demo: DevOps - Version Control, Deployment Modes, REST API
videoDescription:
Ignition 8.3 introduces powerful DevOps capabilities that make it easier than ever to version, deploy, and manage your Ignition systems. Inductive Automation Chief Technology Evangelist Travis Cox explains how the new file-based configuration model enables seamless Git integration, how Deployment Modes support multiple environment setups, and how the new built-in REST API gives external tools full access to the Ignition Gateway.

FEATURE HIGHLIGHTS
- All Ignition configuration files are now accessible in the file system
- Native Git version control support for projects and gateway configuration
- Environmental overrides with deployment modes
- REST API is now built into Ignition’s gateway web UI
- Full API docs are available via OpenAPI
- Role-based access control for secure API interaction

LIVE DEMO HIGHLIGHTS
- Initializing Git in the data directory and pushing to GitLab
- Viewing JSON project and configuration files directly in Ignition folders
- Making changes and tracking them through GitHub Desktop
- Creating Deployment Modes for dev/test/prod with configuration inheritance
- Viewing mode-specific overrides in Git and the file system
- Using the REST API with tools like Postman and Somnia
- Viewing, adding, and configuring devices and DB connections through the API
- Accessing gateway metrics programmatically through API calls
- Dynamically creating new devices through a POST request

channelDescription:
📱CANAL VALOR é a sua porta de entrada para o mundo de produtos digitais.

Quer aprender sobre gestão de produto, roadmap, backlog, estratégia de produto e agilidade? No CANAL VALOR você encontra muito conteúdo e confere toda quinta e sábado às 20h uma aula especial ao vivo sobre gestão de produtos digitais.

👨‍🦲 Quem é Marcelo Neves?

Marcelo Neves é o fundador do CANAL VALOR e trabalha com liderança em desenvolvimento de produtos digitais desde 1993. Nos anos 90 liderou o desenvolvimento de produtos B2C voltados para a saúde.  Nos anos 2000 esteve envolvido na liderança de produtos para indústrias farmacêuticas, produtos de automação de força de vendas e de gestão de relacionamento com clientes (CRM). Em todos esses anos participou como desenvolvedor, líder de time de produto, mentor e empreendedor na construção de produtos digitais, atendendo empresas como IBGE, Sanofi Aventis, Pfizer, Yduqs, Banco Votorantin e vários outras.

videoTitle: Streamlining Power BI with DevOps: Automating Testing, CICD & Analytics Delivery - Datagaps Webinar
videoDescription:
In this Datagaps webinar, discover how modern *Power BI DevOps* practices help enterprise teams automate testing, integrate CI/CD pipelines, and deliver trusted analytics faster.

As Power BI environments scale, manual validation and traditional deployment methods create risks across development, testing, and production environments. This session explains how organizations can implement automated Power BI testing, DevOps workflows, and continuous analytics delivery to ensure dashboard accuracy, reliability, and faster releases.

videoTitle: What is GitOps? The DevOps Method Everyone Is Talking About
videoDescription:
What is GitOps? In this video, we break down GitOps — the DevOps method everyone is talking about — and explain how it helps teams manage infrastructure and deployments using Git as the single source of truth. If you’re curious about how GitOps works in modern DevOps workflows, how it integrates with tools like Kubernetes and CI/CD pipelines, and why so many organizations are adopting GitOps for automation and reliability, this video will give you a simple, beginner-friendly explanation. By the end, you’ll understand the core ideas behind GitOps, why it’s becoming a key DevOps practice, and how it can make deployments faster, safer, and more consistent.

videoTitle: Kubernetes High Availability Made Easy #kubernetes #devops
videoDescription:

You’ll learn:

- How etcd handles leader and follower replicas
- Why controller-manager & scheduler run in standby mode
- How HA works with multiple masters and a load balancer

This is the foundation of reliability in production Kubernetes clusters.

videoTitle: DSI Seminar | Transforming DevOps for Scientific Innovation
videoDescription:
Over the last decade, the Materials Project (MP, materialsproject.org) has evolved from a niche research database into the world's leading open-access platform for materials data, now serving a rapidly growing community of over 700,000 registered users. Supporting this exponential growth while managing over hundreds of terabytes of scientific data required a fundamental architectural shift from on-premises hardware to a resilient, cloud-native ecosystem. In this session, Dr. Huck detailed the engineering strategy behind MP’s modernization. He provided a technical “under-the-hood” look at how his team leverages AWS CloudFormation for Infrastructure-as-Code; utilizes Kong API Gateway to orchestrate traffic across containerized microservices; and achieves end-to-end observability with Datadog. The talk covered practical lessons in scaling scientific infrastructure, including the implementation of blue/green deployments for backend services and the strategic use of the AWS Open Data Program to democratize access to materials data.

Dr. Patrick Huck is a Senior Computing Engineer (CSE5) at Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory, where he serves as the Technical Lead for the Materials Project. He is the chief platform architect behind what is widely known as the “Google of Materials”—a massive open-access platform that harnesses supercomputing to predict material properties, helping over 700,000 researchers worldwide bypass years of trial-and-error in designing next-generation materials. Dr. Huck holds a Ph.D. in High Energy Nuclear Physics and brings over 14 years of experience bridging the gap between complex research requirements and advanced computing infrastructure. Beyond his work on the Materials Project, he plays a key strategic role in the DOE’s newly launched Genesis Mission. As the L3 Co-Lead for the American Science Cloud’s Container Services & Platform, he is currently helping to design the unified, national-scale infrastructure that will connect the DOE’s most powerful supercomputers into a single, AI-ready ecosystem.

LLNL-VIDEO-2020324
channelDescription: For 70 years, Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory has leveraged science and technology to make the world a safer place. Check out events, webinars, workshops and more that support the Lab's mission.

videoTitle: ZeroOps: Building AI-Powered No-Code DevOps on Google Cloud | Drag-n-Drop Infrastructure Automation
videoDescription:
Try It Out : https://zerops.tropicalcoders.xyz
ZeroOps is a no code DevOps automation and infrastructure orchestration platform that enables users to visually design server architectures through a drag and drop interface while abstracting the underlying operational complexity. The platform leverages advanced Google Cloud technologies including Google ADK and Gemini for intelligent requirement analysis architecture reasoning and automated infrastructure synthesis. Based on the user designed or agent generated architecture ZeroOps dynamically produces production grade Terraform code aligned with Google Cloud reference architectures and best practices. The system integrates deeply with core Google Cloud services such as Compute Engine GKE Cloud Run VPC Cloud Load Balancing Cloud IAM and Artifact Registry to enable secure scalable and highly available deployments. An agentic recommendation engine analyzes workload characteristics performance constraints and cost requirements to generate optimized architectures while a dedicated cost optimization agent applies resource right sizing pricing analysis and deployment efficiency strategies. ZeroOps also includes a cloud migration and correction engine capable of analyzing legacy or faulty Terraform configurations and refactoring them into optimized cloud ready infrastructure for target Google Cloud environments. The platform enforces security observability and compliance by default through native integration with Security Command Center Cloud Monitoring Cloud Logging and policy driven validation mechanisms delivering an end to end DevOps lifecycle covering design deployment optimization scaling and governance within a unified Google Cloud ecosystem.

videoTitle: Kubernetes Troubleshooting in Production: Real DevOps Debugging with Services, DNS & Netshoot
videoDescription:
In this hands-on Kubernetes troubleshooting session, we debug a realistic production-style deployment where everything looks healthy — Pods are running, Services are created, Deployments are green — yet the application does not work.

This lecture demonstrates real-world DevOps debugging techniques including:

Identifying Kubernetes Service port mismatches

Verifying traffic flow using Test-NetConnection / Telnet

Debugging from inside a Pod using kubectl exec

Understanding minimal container images

Using Netshoot for advanced network troubleshooting

Fixing cross-namespace DNS resolution issues

Testing database connectivity with Netcat (nc)

Differentiating ICMP vs TCP port checks

If you support Kubernetes in production, these troubleshooting skills are essential.

Perfect for: DevOps Engineers, SREs, Platform Engineers, Cloud Engineers, and Kubernetes administrators.

⏱️ Timestamps

00:00 – Introduction & System Overview
03:30 – Deploying the Challenge & Verifying Cluster State
07:00 – Service Connectivity Issue & Port Mismatch Debugging
10:30 – Application Loads but Database Connection Fails
14:00 – Debugging Inside the Pod & Missing Tools Problem
17:30 – Using Netshoot for DNS & Cross-Namespace Debugging
21:00 – Testing Database Port Connectivity with Netcat
24:30 – Understanding ICMP vs TCP & Structured Troubleshooting Approach

videoTitle: New 📚 Release! Architecting and Implementing DevOps for Infrastructure Management in Azure #books
videoDescription:
New 📚 Release! Architecting and Implementing DevOps for Infrastructure Management in Azure: Infrastructure DevOps in Azure: Configure, Deploy, & Automate Your Azure resources with terraform, GitHub Actions and Ansible by Ashish Raj

Embark on a transformative journey from basic to intricate infrastructure configurations—think high availability, scalability, and security—all through the power of Infrastructure as Code. Dive into the automation of Azure resource deployments, leveraging DevOps tools with comprehensive, step-by-step guidance and practical examples. But the learning doesn't stop at deployment; delve deeper into automated governance and monitoring, ensuring your infrastructure is not just built but also managed with precision and efficiency.

Find it on Leanpub!

#MicrosoftAzure #coding #programming #newreleases #books #bookstan

Link: https://leanpub.com/devopsinazure

About the Book

In the face of accelerated development cycles, Infrastructure teams must align with the culture of rapid deployment and infrastructure versioning. Embracing Infrastructure as Code, Continuous Integration, and Configuration Management practices becomes imperative. Tools like Terraform, GitHub Actions, and Ansible empower infrastructure engineers to implement scalable Infrastructure as Code practices in Azure.

This book serves as a thorough guide for deploying, configuring, and automating Azure infrastructure using DevOps tools. It navigates through cloud architecture patterns with DevOps tools, illustrating their application in Azure solution design. Subsequently, the book delves into the implementation of the architected solution, utilizing DevOps tools like Terraform, GitHub Actions, Packer, Ansible, etc., to address specific architectural requirements with emphasis on scaling the solution architecture and establishing governance, aligning with DevOps principles.

Upon finishing this book, readers will be equipped with the expertise to transform business requirements into Azure-compatible solution architectures, seamlessly incorporating a DevOps approach to deployment. Readers will demonstrate proficiency in executing end-to-end deployment with governance and monitoring proficiently through the consistent application of DevOps tools.

About the Author

Ashish Raj is a technologist and storyteller who helps engineering teams improve their velocity. He believes the biggest challenges facing engineers aren't technical, but not knowing how to tie people, process and technology. With an extensive background spanning innovative startups to leading tech conglomerates, Ashish currently serves as a Cloud & DevOps Architect at Haufe Group.

A fervent advocate and contributor to the DevOps and cloud communities, he shares expertise through webinars and local meetups. Holding Microsoft certifications in DevOps, Architecture, and Certified Microsoft Trainer. Ashish co-founded "AzureTalk," a prominent IM-based global Azure community on Telegram, and initiated "AzureDevOpsPro," a thriving DevOps community. He shares educational cloud and DevOps videos on his YouTube channel, youtube.com/ashishrajsrivastava

channelDescription:
LaunchDarkly is the runtime control layer for the AI era. For over a decade we have helped engineering teams control what ships in production, from feature flags to full release governance. Today that mission extends to the AI agents and code that are increasingly building, modifying, and operating software on behalf of engineering teams.

With CodeControl and AgentControl, teams can ship faster, govern what runs in production, and automatically respond when something needs to change, without waiting on a redeploy. LaunchDarkly runs on infrastructure that handles over 50 trillion flag evaluations a day, which means the same platform that governs your features also governs your agents.

Founded in 2014 by Edith Harbaugh and John Kodumal, LaunchDarkly has been named on the Forbes Cloud 100 list and serves some of the most demanding engineering teams in the world.

Learn more at launchdarkly.com

videoTitle: DevOps Hands-On | Ansible – Day 4  ( DevOpsFarm Day 46 )
videoDescription:
In this in-depth session, we dive into the critical world of DevOps monitoring and master the industry-leading open-source tools: Grafana, Prometheus, and Loki.

Learn why monitoring is essential for getting system feedback, taking proactive action, and preventing system failure due to issues like high CPU usage, low RAM, or disk space depletion. We cover the key aspects you must monitor, including system resources, logs, and traces.




What you will learn in this session:


The Importance of Monitoring: Understand the benefits, including anomaly detection, generating alerts, and creating useful dashboards for historical data analysis.



Core Monitoring Tools: An overview of popular tools like Grafana, New Relic, Datadog, and Dynatrace.



Prometheus Explained: A deep dive into Prometheus, the time-series database used for collecting and tracking metrics (like CPU usage over time).



Loki for Logging: Learn how Loki is used for log collection and parsing, making logs consumable by Grafana.



The Grafana Stack in Action: See how Grafana serves as the visualization layer, consuming data from Prometheus and Loki to create powerful dashboards and configure alerts.



Practical Demo: We demonstrate connecting data sources, exploring metrics using PromQL, and setting up label-based notification policies to ensure the right teams are alerted instantly.


Whether you are a beginner or a seasoned DevOps professional, this guide provides the knowledge and practical steps needed to implement robust monitoring for any application or microservice environment.

videoTitle: How to explain Grafana and Prometheus Stack deployment in Production in DevOps SRE interviews?
videoDescription:
Support my work: https://buymeacoffee.com/techapricate

In this video, I explain how to confidently describe the Grafana + Prometheus observability and monitoring stack deployment in production during DevOps and SRE interviews.

You’ll learn how Terraform modules stored in GitLab can be used to deploy the kube-prometheus-stack Helm chart for a complete Observability setup. I’ll walk you through how Prometheus collects application and cluster-level metrics from AWS EKS clusters, integrates with Prometheus Mimir for long-term storage, and how Grafana is configured with Mimir as a data source and PostgreSQL as a backend database.

This explanation will help you stand out in interviews by showing practical knowledge of real-world production-grade observability setups, from infrastructure as code (IaC) to monitoring architecture design.

🧠 What You’ll Learn:
How to explain Prometheus and Grafana observability architecture in interviews
How to use Terraform + GitLab for deploying kube-prometheus-stack
Integration of Prometheus with Mimir for scalable metric storage
How Grafana connects to Mimir as a data source and uses a PostgreSQL backend
How metrics are collected from EKS clusters and workloads via Kubernetes APIs

Best practices to explain Monitoring, Alerting, and Visualization in production

🚀 Technologies Covered:
Terraform | GitLab CI/CD | Prometheus | Grafana | Prometheus Mimir | PostgreSQL | kube-prometheus-stack | Helm | AWS EKS | Kubernetes Observability | Cloud Monitoring

videoTitle: Ways to make money (#4): Cloud and DevOps  #techcareers #careeradvice
videoDescription:
Cloud and DevOps engineers keep production alive when outages, bad deploys, and latency spikes hit. From Linux and Docker to Kubernetes, Terraform, AWS, Azure, CI/CD, dashboards, alerts, and SRE workflows, this is one of the highest-pressure paths in tech.

If you want a high-paying tech career, cloud engineering, DevOps, and site reliability engineering offer strong salaries, real demand, and a fast path in for builders who can automate and stay calm in chaos. The trade-off: on-call stress, constant change, and invisible work when systems run smoothly.

Follow for more high-income tech career breakdowns, software engineering salary facts, and real-world job paths.

videoTitle: DevOps Myths Everyone Gets Wrong!
videoDescription:
A lot of people think they understand DevOps but so many still get it wrong 😅.

In this short video, I’m breaking down the biggest DevOps misconceptions that confuse developers, IT teams, and tech leads everywhere.

If you’ve ever heard someone say “DevOps is just about tools” or “we’ll just hire a DevOps engineer and boom, we’re DevOps” — this one’s for you.

I’ll explain:
- Why DevOps isn’t about tools like Docker, Kubernetes, or Jenkins
- Why you can’t outsource DevOps culture
- And how DevOps connects developers and operations to work faster, smarter, and with fewer surprises.

Whether you’re new to DevOps or already deep in the cloud-native world, this short will help you finally understand what DevOps really is — and what it’s not.

👉 If you found this helpful, like the video, subscribe, and drop a comment telling me which DevOps myth you’ve heard the most.

27:07 Why stakeholder management is a core DevOps skill.
32:43 How AI changes DevOps and the case for ClickOps.

Cortex co-founder and CTO Ganesh Datta sits down with Matt Bailey, DevOps consultant and founder of Merge Ready. Matt shares lessons from helping large regulated organizations in finance, healthcare, and government transform their DevOps practices, and explains why DevOps is an outcome rather than a toolchain.

Matt and Ganesh discuss why compliance can be mostly automated rather than a mandatory bottleneck, how to turn 30-day approval processes into continuous audit readiness through controls as code, and why treating platform teams as product teams drives natural adoption. They also explore decision latency as a core organizational problem, the importance of stakeholder management as a DevOps skill, and how AI agents may shift infrastructure drift management from prevention to embrace.

videoTitle: Is your multicloud pipeline compliant? DevOps vs GitOps
videoDescription:
DevOps Isn't Enough: GitOps Strategies for Regulated Multicloud Deployments

Sujeily Fonseca-Gonzalez walks through how GitOps practices and Argo CD make multicloud compliance manageable for regulated environments like FedRAMP, HIPAA, and Protected B. Learn how git becomes your audit trail, how evidence lockers automate proof of compliance, and how Argo CD's built-in RBAC, sync policies, and audit logging map to regulated deployment requirements across AWS, IBM, and Google Cloud.

Summary: 
Your project's direct dependencies are the easy part. Viral Chhasatia walks through dependency auditing across Python, npm, Maven, Go, Rust, and Docker using tools like pip licenses, CycloneDX, npm license checker, go licenses, cargo about, and Sift. Learn why transitive dependencies carry hidden license risks, why your SBOM is already outdated, and why no single tool gives you the complete picture.

Session: Bridging DevOps And GitOps: MultiCloud Strategies For HyperScalers

GitOps has transformed the way organizations manage cloud-native applications, but scaling it across federal and commercial hyperscalers presents unique challenges. In this session, Sujeily Fonseca-Gonzalez will share lessons learned from leading GitOps implementations in highly regulated environments, covering best practices for security, compliance, automation, and scalability. Attendees will gain insights into key differences between federal and commercial cloud GitOps workflows, overcoming security and compliance hurdles in highly regulated environments, enhancing CI/CD pipelines for resilience, observability, and automation, and lessons from real-world implementations, including multi-cloud strategies and open source technologies.



Learn more about GitOps strategies for complex multi-cloud deployment environments. Sujeily Fonseca-Gonzalez explains how to manage infrastructure consistency across hyperscalers.

This session breaks down the technical transition from DevOps to GitOps, specifically addressing the challenges of tool fragmentation and security in distributed systems. If you are an engineer or architect managing infrastructure, this overview provides clarity on maintaining compliance while scaling operations across different platforms.

Learn to overcome common hurdles when implementing GitOps strategies at scale. The discussion covers practical lessons from real-world multi-cloud deployment scenarios, focusing on how to maintain security standards and operational consistency. By the end, you will understand the necessary architectural shifts required for successful cloud infrastructure management.

videoTitle: The DevOps Godfather on AI's "Dark Factory" Problem
videoDescription:
Patrick Debois — the "godfather of DevOps" who coined the term back in 2009 — says we're living through the same skepticism all over again, except this time it's about coding agents. He makes the case that "the dark factory" isn't about better prompts or bigger models. It's about rebuilding how teams, platforms, and entire orgs are structured around autonomous agents.

What we cover:
– Why organizations resist the "dark factory" model of autonomous coding agents
– How team dynamics shift as developers become agent orchestrators
– Two metrics that actually measure agentic productivity
– The platform team's growing role: skill registries, guardrails, and paved roads
– How to hire and interview for AI-native engineering roles
– Making the business case for agent-driven engineering investment

videoTitle: How dbForge SQL Complete is Involved in the Database DevOps Process
videoDescription:
⚡ In this part of the video, we’ll look into how specifically dbForge SQL Complete takes part in the database development process. The dbForge SQL Complete is a powerful IntelliSense-style SQL completion, code refactoring, code formatting tool for SQL Server. SQL Complete is available as an add-in for Visual Studio and SQL Server Management Studio.

ℹ️ In the automated CI process, SQL Complete serves as a means to format update or database creation scripts before they get into a NuGet package.
When database development is in the hot phase, developers often tend to forget about formatting a script before sending the UPDATE scripts to source control (Git) or use different formatting tools with inconsistent settings.

As a result, the script formatting in NuGet looks like a mess that has nothing in common with the corporate code formatting standards.
dbForge SQL Complete is a robust tool which can help developers write and format code while saving time on these processes.

videoTitle: Cracking the DevOps Code: Courses & Certifications for Success
videoDescription:
Take the courses mentioned here, get certifications and do not forget to add these using our AI-Powered Resume Builder (https://pro.kudoswall.com)
Certifications for Employed DevOps Professionals:
AWS Certified DevOps Engineer: This certification from Amazon Web Services (AWS) validates your expertise in deploying, operating, and managing applications on the AWS platform.
Azure DevOps Engineer: Offered by Microsoft, this certification demonstrates your skills in designing and implementing DevOps practices using Azure technologies.
Google Cloud Professional DevOps Engineer: This certification focuses on designing, developing, and managing DevOps practices on the Google Cloud Platform.
Certified Kubernetes Administrator (CKA): Certified by the Cloud Native Computing Foundation (CNCF), CKA validates your Kubernetes skills, which are crucial for container orchestration.
Certified Jenkins Engineer: Offered by CloudBees, this certification recognizes your proficiency in using Jenkins for continuous integration and continuous delivery (CI/CD) pipelines.
Red Hat Certified Engineer in DevOps: This certification from Red Hat validates your skills in using Red Hat technologies for automating, managing, and optimizing DevOps processes.

Courses for Aspiring DevOps Engineers:
Introduction to DevOps Practices: Online platforms like Coursera, edX, and Udemy offer various introductory courses that cover the fundamental concepts and practices of DevOps.
Docker and Kubernetes Courses: Understanding containerization and orchestration tools like Docker and Kubernetes is essential. There are numerous courses available for beginners and advanced users.
Cloud Platform Courses (AWS, Azure, GCP): Learning the basics of cloud platforms and their DevOps-related services is crucial. Many platforms offer free and paid courses for beginners.
CI/CD Pipeline Courses: Courses on continuous integration and continuous delivery tools like Jenkins, GitLab CI/CD, and CircleCI are essential for automating software delivery.
Infrastructure as Code (IaC) Courses: Learning tools like Terraform and Ansible for automating infrastructure deployment is key for DevOps engineers.
Monitoring and Logging Courses: Understanding tools like Prometheus, Grafana, and ELK Stack (Elasticsearch, Logstash, Kibana) for monitoring and logging is vital.
Scripting and Programming Courses: Proficiency in scripting languages like Python and programming languages like Java can enhance your DevOps skills.
Security and Compliance Courses: DevOps engineers should also be well-versed in security practices. Courses on DevSecOps and security automation can be beneficial.
#DevOpsIndia #Certifications #Courses #TechSkills #CareerGrowth #Cloud #Automation

videoDescription:

videoTitle: AWS re:Invent 2021 - Covert ops on DevOps: Leveraging security to shift left
videoDescription:
Working successfully with the cloud requires both effective security and seamless automation. Developers and DevOps teams know this all too well as they launch new applications into the cloud while trying to thwart unapproved third parties. In this session, learn from cloud security experts how you can secure your automation and automate your security, and discover best processes for adopting a security as code mindset for ultra-modern architectures. Finally, find out how to stop the most advanced attacks by using new strategies and technologies with fully automated security of the CI/CD pipeline, in the CI/CD pipeline, and across the cloud and application lifecycle. This presentation is brought to you by Check Point Software, an AWS Partner. Speakers: Rolando Panez (Check Point Software) and Yuval Shchory (Check Point Software)

videoTitle: RabbitMQ for DevOps Engineers: Hands-On Demo for Beginners #devops #rabbitmq  #kubernetes
videoDescription:
RabbitMQ for DevOps Engineers: Hands-On Demo for Beginners #devops #rabbitmq  #kubernetes

Github Repo : https://github.com/RohanRusta21/demo_rabbitmq.git

RabbitMQ Official Doc : https://www.rabbitmq.com/docs

Timestamp:
Understand Concept : 00:00
RabbitMQ Hands-on : 22:06

🧠 1. What Is RabbitMQ?

RabbitMQ is an open-source message broker — it acts as a middleman between different parts of a system that need to communicate asynchronously.
It uses the AMQP (Advanced Message Queuing Protocol) as its core protocol.

In simpler terms:

It receives messages from producers (applications sending data),

Stores them temporarily in queues,

And delivers them to consumers (applications receiving data).

This helps decouple systems — the sender and receiver don’t need to be active at the same time.

⚙️ 2. Why Use RabbitMQ?

✅ Decoupling – Producers don’t need to know who consumes messages.
✅ Asynchronous communication – Improves system responsiveness.
✅ Load balancing – Messages can be distributed across multiple consumers.
✅ Reliability – Ensures message delivery even if parts of the system fail.
✅ Scalability – You can add more queues or consumers easily.
✅ Flexible routing – Decide how messages flow using exchanges and routing keys.

🔩 3. Core RabbitMQ Concepts
🏭 Producers

Applications that send messages to RabbitMQ.
They don’t send messages directly to a queue but to an exchange.

🎯 Exchanges

An exchange routes messages to queues based on routing rules.

Exchange types:

Type Behavior Example Use Case
Direct Routes messages to queues whose binding key exactly matches routing key Task queues
Fanout Broadcasts all messages to all queues bound to it Notifications, pub/sub
Topic Routes messages based on pattern matching (* and #) Selective subscriptions
Headers Routes based on header attributes Advanced filtering

videoTitle: Day 29: Monitoring & Logging in Azure DevOps | 30 Days Azure DevOps Challenge 🚀
videoDescription:
👋 Welcome to Day 29 of my 30 Days Azure DevOps Challenge 🚀

Today’s focus: Monitoring & Logging in Azure DevOps 🔍

Monitoring and logging are the backbone of observability in DevOps. While automation ensures smooth deployments, monitoring ensures that systems stay healthy, and logging helps you catch errors before they become big problems.

🔹 What we covered today:

Why monitoring & logging are critical in DevOps

Difference between Monitoring (health & performance) and Logging (detailed events & errors)

Using Azure DevOps pipeline logs to debug failures

Dashboards & reports for performance tracking

Integration with Azure Monitor and Application Insights for full observability

✨ Key takeaway:
Automation doesn’t stop at deployment — it continues with monitoring, logging, and observability for reliable DevOps.

📌 Next in Day 30 → Security & Compliance in Azure DevOps 🔒🚀

👍 Don’t forget to Like, Comment, and Subscribe to follow along with the full 30 Days Challenge!

#AzureDevOps #DevOps #Monitoring #Logging #AzureMonitor #ApplicationInsights #CloudComputing #Automation #30DaysChallenge

videoTitle: What is Git? How would you define Git? || #GitHub #GitLab #BitBucket #DevOps #Git #Python #Java
videoDescription:
What is Git?

How would you define Git?

Well, Git is a distributed version control system, which means two things:

1. It's distributed so people around the world can share code
2. It does version control

So, with version control, you can take snapshots of the state of your current code and maintain a history.

Branching is really the third thing. Git branches allow developers to do isolated development and take chances without messing up the main branch.

So when someone asks me 'What is Git?', that is how I typically respond.

videoTitle: What is Secure DevOps?
videoDescription:
It’s surprisingly easy for hackers to impersonate authorized users of your corporate Git environment. Why? Because your developers can access it using their personal accounts, which are not tied to a corporate identity. That makes it difficult for IT to control access. And because contributors can sign the author field of a commit with anything they like, IT can’t track who is really authoring these commits.

Just as bad, contributors are committing code on unauthorized machines, often without even logging into the Git web service, evading all access controls.

This leaves your software components vulnerable to unauthorized access and tampering, which can lead to malicious code getting distributed to all of your clients.

With Secure DevOps by Beyond Identity, you can ensure that only your developers check in code to your repo. And that all commits came from an authorized user and device. All without impacting software velocity.
channelDescription:
Beyond Identity is the only IAM platform architected to stop unauthorized access. Our mission is to protect the livelihood of our customers, freeing them to focus on what matters. 

Every other IAM platform was built to drive productivity, making security an afterthought and providing a highway for bad actors to exploit. Beyond Identity is the only secure-by-design IAM platform foundationally built to ensure only trusted users and secure devices can ever gain and maintain access.

videoTitle: Your Shell Scripts Are Silent Killers. Fix Them Now. #devops #bash #linux #automation #coding
videoDescription:

Command: set -euo pipefail

videoTitle: CPU Spike at 3 AM? Here's What Catches It #devops #shorts
videoDescription:
Monitoring tools don’t have to be complicated.

In this short video, monitoring tools are explained in the simplest possible way, so even beginners can understand them easily. Think of monitoring tools as health trackers for servers and applications. Just like a smartwatch tracks your heartbeat, monitoring tools track the health of your systems.

Prometheus plays a key role in modern DevOps monitoring. It continuously checks system metrics such as CPU usage, memory, response time, and error rates. This constant monitoring helps teams understand how healthy their infrastructure is at any moment.

When something feels wrong—like a sudden spike in CPU usage or a drop in performance—alerts automatically fire. This allows DevOps teams to act quickly, often fixing issues before users even notice. That’s why monitoring is essential for cloud platforms, corporate systems, retail applications, and large-scale infrastructure.

This video is perfect for DevOps beginners, IT students, cloud engineers, and anyone new to monitoring tools. Simple explanations build strong foundations, and understanding monitoring early will help you grow faster in your DevOps journey.

👉 Subscribe for more easy DevOps explanations, monitoring basics, and real-world tech concepts explained in short videos.
Ref: https://prometheus.io/
